notes.title: Never did she utter one word of complaint.
notes.source: Bahíyyih Khánum and Lady Blomfield, The Chosen Highway, Part II, Chapter I
notes.whySelected: Ásíyih Khánum's exile chronicle won because it turned the search away from report-style Bahá’í News material into a full literary memoir passage centered on a woman’s fortitude: the lost household, Bahá’u’lláh’s imprisonment, the family’s hiding, the winter road to Baghdad, and the repeated tenderness of the Mother of Consolation. It beat the more obvious Bahíyyih Khánum portrait/tribute lane because that protagonist and image already exist in the archive, and it beat Martha Root and modern community candidates because those read more informational or source-warm.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Chosen Highway passage, holy-family exile/imprisonment story, Ásíyih Khánum/Navváb monument image, Bahíyyih-as-narrator selection, winter road to Baghdad, or sacrifice/fortitude-under-persecution arc. Prefer non-bahai-library if strong, non-Holy-Land/non-Persian community material, or a fresh memoir with a lighter emotional register.

notes.title: When are you coming back?
notes.whySelected: The Quilhuay travelling-institute report won because it broke the immediate Memorials/bahai-library/‘Abdu’l-Bahá pressure with a non-Persian, local-community story from Bahá’í News. It has concrete scene work: the rough mountain road, an unoccupied jail used as lodging, battery-powered projectors, mothers and children learning together, Quechua translation, Teodora carrying gear on her back, visits to neighboring villages, and the repeated question, "When are you coming back?" It beat the Maharashtra/Vasantkutti correspondence-course story because the Peru passage had fuller community texture, stronger image fit, and a more complete closing turn.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Bahá’í News community-consolidation report, Peru/Quechua mountain institute, children-and-mothers class scene, or development-report style passage. Prefer a more literary memoir, authoritative book, or woman/local protagonist with direct image fit and less report framing.

notes.title: That star, Nabíl, will shine forever.
notes.whySelected: Nabíl-i-Akbar's memorial won because it restored an authoritative bahai.org source after three bahai-library additions, centered a non-‘Abdu’l-Bahá protagonist, and gave a complete scholar-to-fugitive arc: unmatched learning, recognition of Bahá’u’lláh, public teaching, loss of rank and safety, concealed service, poverty in Bukhárá, and lasting spiritual triumph. It beat Star of the West/Martha Root travel reports because those read more informational than story-shaped, and it beat modern Dorothy Baker material because rights/source-shape were weaker for full verbatim copying.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Memorials of the Faithful entry, another learned Persian male teacher, another fugitive scholar/cleric transformation, Nabíl-i-Akbar image, or devotion/teaching-under-persecution arc. Prefer women/community transformation, non-Persian local community material, or a concrete non-Memorials source with direct image fit.

notes.title: No bride had ever chanted so.
notes.source: Munirih Khanum, Munirih Khanum: Memoirs and Letters, pp. 38-51
notes.whySelected: Munirih Khanum's journey-to-‘Akká and wedding passage won because it opened a fresh autobiographical source lane, centered a woman's own voice, and gave a complete spiritual arc: sea crossing, pearl dream, blocked entry, sudden welcome, renaming as Munirih, and the spare prison-room wedding. It beat the Khadijih Bagum declaration recollection because that overlapped the existing Dawn-Breakers declaration story and House of the Báb image, and it beat lower-prestige anecdote lanes because this source had fuller literary force and direct portrait image fit.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Munirih Khanum memoir passage, ‘Abdu’l-Bahá marriage/holy Household episode, ‘Akká arrival-under-restriction scene, dream-of-guidance passage, or Munírih Khánum portrait. Prefer a non-‘Akká setting, non-bahai-library source if strong, or a community transformation story outside holy-family memoir material.

notes.title: I am being awakened and bathed.
notes.source: Agnes Baldwin Alexander, History of the Bahá'í Faith in Japan 1914-1938, chapter 3, pp. 13-14
notes.whySelected: Kikutaro Fukuta's first letter from Japan to ‘Abdu'l-Bahá won because it opened a fresh Japan/Agnes Alexander source lane and gave a complete, humble awakening scene in the young student's own translated words. It beat Keith Ransom-Kehler arrival material, Bahiyyih Khanum exile recollections, and broader Martha Root/Japan reports because it was less source-warm, more compact, directly imageable, and spiritually intimate without repeating recent ‘Abdu’l-Bahá child/service or race-unity patterns.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Japan first-believer item, Agnes Alexander history passage, Esperanto/Tokyo teaching-opening vignette, or short first-letter/first-convert awakening scene. Prefer a different region or a fuller community transformation arc from a non-Bahá’í World, non-Portals source.

notes.title: A black rose and a black sweet.
notes.source: Howard Colby Ives, Portals to Freedom, chapter 4, pp. 60-68
notes.whySelected: The Bowery boys episode won because it refreshed the source mix with Portals to Freedom while delivering a complete, memorable race-unity scene: ‘Abdu’l-Bahá welcomes an anxious Black child, changes how the other boys see him, and leaves Ives shaken by love and spiritual beauty. It beat modern community-report candidates and ordinary Promulgation talk passages because it was concrete, literary, self-contained, and highly imageable.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another ‘Abdu’l-Bahá-and-children scene, Bowery/New York race-unity vignette, candy/sweetness kindness episode, Portals to Freedom excerpt, or generic Promulgation-adjacent talk. Prefer a non-‘Abdu’l-Bahá protagonist, women/community transformation outside Western pilgrimage settings, or a non-Western source with a fuller arc.

notes.title: The scar of friendship will never be healed.
notes.source: William and Marguerite Sears, "Black Sunlight," Bahá’í World, Vol. 12, pp. 926-927
notes.whySelected: The Kampala incident won because it was short, complete, unusually literary, and emotionally memorable: a young Buganda moves from bewilderment to a heart-deep friendship with Bahá’u’lláh. It accepted a Bahá’í World repetition penalty because the protagonist, African setting, and understanding/friendship register were fresh against recent ‘Abdu’l-Bahá danger, service, martyrdom, and teaching-method lanes.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another Bahá’í World item, William Sears/Africa travel reminiscence, Kampala/Uganda House of Worship image, simple-conversion/understanding vignette, or 'friendship of the heart' quote. Prefer a non-Bahá’í World source with women/community transformation, Promulgation only if concrete and story-shaped, or an authorized memoir outside recent source families.

notes.title: Please God this dynamite will not explode.
notes.source: Shoghi Effendi, God Passes By, chapter XVII
notes.whySelected: The passage answered the Covenant/providence target with a complete, dramatic arc: the second commission arrives in ‘Akká, the ship appears ready to carry ‘Abdu’l-Bahá away, then turns toward Constantinople and the danger is providentially averted. It beat Shamsu’d-Duhá from Memorials because that runner-up, while vivid, substantially overlapped the existing Ṭáhirih Memorials entry and had weaker direct image fit.
notes.avoidNext: Do not follow immediately with another ‘Abdu’l-Bahá imprisonment-in-‘Akká passage, ship/deportation providence scene, Covenant-breaker crisis from God Passes By chapter XVII, or Acre Prison image. Prefer a non-‘Abdu’l-Bahá protagonist, women/community transformation outside Memorials overlap, or an authorized memoir with a fresh emotional register.
